However, the logs show the service is attempting to connect to the literal string `$[env:THIRD_PARTY_URL]` rather than the configured URL value. The engineer checks the reserved prefix list and confirms THIRD_PARTY_URL does not start with any reserved prefix.
questions.question: What is the most likely cause of the literal placeholder appearing at runtime instead of the resolved value?

options.label: A
options.text: The variable was set as a pipeline variable rather than an environment variable. Pipeline variables are not available to the OSGi runtime.

options.label: B
options.text: Secret-type variables cannot be read back in the Cloud Manager UI, so the engineer only thinks the variable is defined. The variable was never actually saved.

options.label: C
options.text: The variable THIRD_PARTY_URL was defined after the environment was last started. The value takes effect immediately for new requests but the OSGi config placeholder does not re-resolve until the next restart.

options.label: D
options.text: The OSGi config file is using the $[env:VARIABLE_NAME] placeholder syntax but the variable was set without a default value and it was not yet propagated to the environment when the bundle resolved. The current likely cause is that the variable was added while a pipeline was actively running and the update was blocked.

questions.rationale: D is the most correct answer. Cloud Manager prevents adding or updating environment/pipeline variables while a pipeline is actively running — the variable management operation is blocked and may silently appear to succeed in the UI while not actually being saved [2]. If the dev environment was freshly provisioned and a non-production pipeline ran during setup, any variable set during that window would be blocked. The literal placeholder `$[env:VARIABLE_NAME]` appears at runtime specifically when the variable is not defined for the environment AND no default value is specified in the OSGi config — the system outputs the literal placeholder string rather than null or an empty string [3]. Option A is plausible but the scenario states the engineer confirmed the variable is defined as an environment variable (visible in the Environments > Configuration tab), not a pipeline variable, making this an incorrect fit. Pipeline variables are set per-pipeline and are not visible in the Environments configuration UI at all [2][4]. Option B is plausible — secrets cannot be read back after creation [1] — but the scenario does not indicate this is a secret-type variable. The engineer viewing the variable in Cloud Manager implies it is a plain (non-secret) environment variable, which IS visible in the UI after creation. Option C is incorrect. Cloud Manager environment variable changes take effect immediately — no restart is required [1]. The OSGi config placeholder resolves at startup but environment variable changes do not require a pod restart to propagate in AEMaaCS. The correct diagnosis points to the variable not having been persisted in the first place due to the pipeline lock.

questions.topic: Cloud Manager API Endpoints and Environment Provisioning
questions.scenario: A platform engineering team is building a self-service portal that allows project teams to provision new AEM development environments on demand. The portal calls the Cloud Manager API using an OAuth Server-to-Server credential. When creating environments, the API call to create a new development environment succeeds, but when the team tries to set environment-specific variables on the freshly created environment via PATCH /api/program/{programId}/environment/{environmentId}/variables, the call returns HTTP 403. The technical account is assigned the Deployment Manager product profile. The portal then attempts to trigger a non-production pipeline on the new environment, and that call also returns 403.
questions.question: Which single root cause most likely explains both 403 failures?

options.label: A
options.text: The Deployment Manager profile only grants access to production environments. Managing development environment variables and non-production pipelines requires the Developer product profile.

options.label: B
options.text: The access token was generated before the new environment was created. Environment-scoped API operations require a fresh token that was issued after the environment ID was registered in IMS.

options.label: C
options.text: The x-gw-ims-org-id header contains the Cloud Manager program ID instead of the IMS Organization ID from the Adobe Developer Console. This causes all environment-scoped and pipeline-scoped calls to fail with 403.

options.label: D
options.text: Creating an environment requires the Business Owner role, and since the technical account is only Deployment Manager, the environment was created under a different program scope that the token cannot access.

questions.rationale: C is correct. A well-documented implementation mistake is confusing the x-gw-ims-org-id header value (the IMS Organization ID from the Adobe Developer Console credentials screen, a constant GUID-format string that identifies the customer's Adobe organization) with the Cloud Manager program ID (a numeric integer visible in Cloud Manager URLs). The IMS Org ID never changes across programs or environments within an organization. Using the program ID as the x-gw-ims-org-id value means the token appears valid (401 would not occur) but the authorization check against the actual organization fails, producing 403 on every endpoint that requires organization-level scope validation — which includes both environment variable management and pipeline execution [1][8]. This would consistently affect all environment-scoped and pipeline-scoped calls after the simpler environment creation call, which succeeds using the HAL link from the root programs response before the org-scoped check is applied. A is incorrect. The Deployment Manager role is the appropriate role for both setting environment variables and triggering pipeline executions. It is not restricted to production environments — Deployment Manager covers both production and non-production operations including environment variable management [5][6]. B is incorrect. OAuth access tokens for the Cloud Manager API are scoped to the organization and the assigned product profiles — they are not scoped to specific environment IDs. A token does not need to be regenerated after an environment is created; any valid token for the correct organization and role can operate on any environment within that program [2]. D is incorrect. While it is true that creating environments requires the Business Owner role [2], the scenario states the environment creation call succeeds — so either the technical account has Business Owner rights or a different account performed the creation. The question asks about the 403 on variable management and pipeline triggering, which is a separate issue from environment creation.

questions.scenario: A developer is debugging a Java NullPointerException that was reported by QA on the Stage Publish tier in AEM as a Cloud Service. The error occurred in the last 30 minutes. The developer wants to see the live log stream as QA reproduces the issue without waiting for a file download.
questions.question: Which tool and command should the developer use to view logs in real time?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the Cloud Manager web UI, navigate to the Environments card, and select 'Download Logs' for the Publish service.

options.label: B
options.text: SSH into the Publish container and run `tail -f /crx-quickstart/logs/error.log`.

options.label: C
options.text: Run `aio cloudmanager:tail-log <ENV_ID> publish aemerror` using the Adobe I/O CLI.

options.label: D
options.text: Open the AEM Web Console at `/system/console/slinglog` and enable real-time log streaming.

questions.rationale: C is correct because real-time log streaming (tailing) in AEMaaCS is only available via the Adobe I/O CLI with the Cloud Manager plugin, using the `aio cloudmanager:tail-log` command [2]. This streams `aemerror` (the Java application error log) from the specified service in real time. A is incorrect because the Cloud Manager UI supports only file downloads — not live streaming. B is incorrect because AEMaaCS runs containerized pods that customers cannot SSH into; direct file system access is not available in cloud environments. D is incorrect because the Sling Log Support console is available only on local SDK instances, not in AEMaaCS cloud environments, and does not provide real-time streaming to the developer's terminal.

questions.scenario: An AEM DevOps engineer is investigating a reported issue where certain page URLs appear not to be cached by the Dispatcher on the Publish tier. They want to confirm whether the Dispatcher is rejecting cache writes for those URLs and identify the reason.
questions.question: Which log file should the engineer examine first?

options.label: A
options.text: The `aemaccess` log on the Publish service, which records all HTTP requests reaching AEM.

options.label: B
options.text: The `aemdispatcher` log on the Dispatcher service, which records cache include/exclude decisions.

options.label: C
options.text: The `aemerror` log on the Publish service, which captures all Java application-level messages.

options.label: D
options.text: The `httpdaccess` log on the Author service, which shows all inbound HTTP requests.

questions.rationale: B is correct because the `aemdispatcher` log is specifically the Dispatcher module log and is the primary source for debugging cache behavior — it records which URLs are cached and which are excluded and why [2]. A is incorrect because `aemaccess` only shows requests that reached the AEM application layer; requests served from the Dispatcher cache never appear in `aemaccess`, so it would not reveal Dispatcher-level cache decisions. C is incorrect because `aemerror` captures Java application exceptions, not Dispatcher cache logic. D is incorrect on two counts: Dispatcher logs exist only on the Publish tier, not Author, and `httpdaccess` is the Apache HTTPD access log — useful for request counting but not for Dispatcher cache include/exclude decisions.

questions.scenario: An enterprise organization running AEM as a Cloud Service must retain all AEM application and Dispatcher logs for 90 days to meet compliance requirements. Cloud Manager retains logs for only 7 days. The team wants to forward logs to their existing Splunk HEC (HTTP Event Collector) endpoint. A security requirement mandates that the Splunk authentication token must never be stored in plaintext in the Git repository.
questions.question: What is the correct approach to configure log forwarding while satisfying the token security requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Store the Splunk token in a `.env` file in the root of the repository and reference it in `logForwarding.yaml` using standard YAML environment variable syntax.

options.label: B
options.text: Create a Cloud Manager Secret Environment Variable for the token and reference it in `logForwarding.yaml` using `${{VARIABLE_NAME}}` syntax, then deploy via a targeted Config pipeline.

options.label: C
options.text: Request Adobe Support to configure Splunk forwarding at the infrastructure level so that no YAML configuration in the repository is needed.

options.label: D
options.text: Base64-encode the Splunk token and store it directly in `logForwarding.yaml` under the `token` field.

questions.rationale: B is correct because AEMaaCS log forwarding uses `logForwarding.yaml` deployed via a Cloud Manager Config pipeline, and secrets such as Splunk HEC tokens must be stored as Cloud Manager Secret Environment Variables and referenced using `${{VARIABLE_NAME}}` syntax [3]. The Secret variable type is never exposed in logs or UI and satisfies the requirement of not storing tokens in plaintext in Git. A is incorrect because `.env` files are a local development pattern; Cloud Manager pipelines do not read `.env` files from the repository, and any file committed to Git containing a token violates the plaintext storage requirement. C is incorrect because self-service log forwarding via `logForwarding.yaml` was introduced specifically to remove the requirement for Adobe Support involvement; organizations can configure it themselves through the Config pipeline. D is incorrect because Base64 encoding is not encryption — it is trivially reversible and does not protect the token; storing it in the YAML file committed to Git is functionally equivalent to plaintext.

questions.scenario: A retail company is running AEM as a Cloud Service and already has a contract with Akamai for CDN services. Their Akamai configuration handles geo-based routing and WAF rules that their security team has already approved. They want to continue using Akamai as their primary CDN while also taking advantage of Adobe's AEMaaCS delivery. The engineering lead asks whether they can replace Adobe's built-in Fastly CDN with Akamai.
questions.question: Which statement accurately describes the correct architecture for this requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: Customers can replace Adobe's Fastly CDN with their own CDN by disabling the Adobe CDN in Cloud Manager and pointing their DNS directly to the Dispatcher tier.

options.label: B
options.text: Akamai can be placed in front of Adobe's Fastly CDN (BYOCDN), but the customer CDN must pass the `X-AEM-Edge-Key` header to authenticate requests to Adobe's CDN. Adobe's Fastly CDN cannot be removed.

options.label: C
options.text: Customers can configure Adobe's Fastly CDN to delegate all routing decisions to Akamai by setting an origin selector in `cdn.yaml` that points to the Akamai ingress domain.

options.label: D
options.text: BYOCDN is supported only for the Author tier; the Publish tier must continue to use Adobe's Fastly CDN exclusively.

questions.rationale: B is correct because Adobe's Fastly CDN is always present and cannot be removed or bypassed in AEMaaCS [1]. Customers who want to use their own CDN (BYOCDN) place it in front of Fastly — they do not replace it. The customer CDN must forward three critical headers: `Host`, `X-Forwarded-Host`, and `X-AEM-Edge-Key`, where the `X-AEM-Edge-Key` is a shared secret configured in `cdn.yaml` that authenticates the customer CDN to Adobe's Fastly layer [5]. A is incorrect because there is no option in Cloud Manager to disable the Adobe CDN; it is a mandatory architectural layer. C is incorrect because origin selectors in `cdn.yaml` route requests from Adobe's CDN to non-AEM backends (like a WordPress blog), not to another CDN — and `.adobeaemcloud.com` domains cannot be used as origins to prevent loops. D has the tiers backwards: BYOCDN is supported for the Publish and Preview tiers but is NOT supported in front of the Author tier, which is the opposite of what option D states.

questions.scenario: A performance engineering team has just activated the New Relic sub-account for their AEM as a Cloud Service production program. The Business Owner clicked 'Activate New Relic' in Cloud Manager. Two days later, the team notices that no APM data has appeared in the New Relic dashboard. The sub-account shows users were added and no one has received an error message.
questions.question: What is the most likely cause of missing APM data, and what action corrects it?

options.label: A
options.text: The New Relic sub-account requires up to 72 hours for Adobe to provision the APM agent; no additional action is needed.

options.label: B
options.text: Users added to the New Relic sub-account have not activated their accounts via the password-reset email; the APM agent will not transmit data until at least one user logs in.

options.label: C
options.text: A Cloud Manager pipeline for the production environment has not been run to successful completion after activation; running a pipeline pushes the activation configuration into the environment and starts the APM agent.

options.label: D
options.text: The New Relic sub-account was shut down due to inactivity; the Business Owner must re-activate it and wait 24 hours.

questions.rationale: C is correct because activating New Relic in the Cloud Manager UI is only the first step. A pipeline run to successful completion is required to push the activation configuration into the target environment and actually start the New Relic APM agent [1]. This is a common failure mode: teams click 'Activate' and wait for data that never arrives because the agent was never deployed. A is incorrect because there is no 72-hour provisioning delay documented for New Relic in AEMaaCS; the issue is a missing pipeline run, not a backend provisioning delay. B is incorrect because user account activation affects whether users can log in to the New Relic UI — it does not affect whether the APM agent on the AEM JVM collects and transmits data. D is incorrect because the 30-day inactivity shutdown would apply to a sub-account that was previously active and then went unused; this account has never had data flowing, so inactivity shutdown is not the cause.

questions.scenario: An AEM as a Cloud Service customer is planning a high-traffic marketing event and asks the DevOps engineer to pre-configure the Publish tier to scale out to at least 10 pods before the event begins. They also want to ensure that pods are not scaled down during the event window. The team has access to Cloud Manager with a Business Owner role.
questions.question: Which action should the DevOps engineer take?

options.label: A
options.text: Log in to Cloud Manager, navigate to the Environments card, and configure a minimum pod count of 10 for the Publish tier using the scaling settings UI.

options.label: B
options.text: Submit an Adobe Support ticket requesting that the orchestration engine be configured to maintain a minimum of 10 publish pods during the event window.

options.label: C
options.text: Use a Cloud Manager pipeline to deploy a Publish tier configuration YAML specifying the target pod count and scale-lock duration.

options.label: D
options.text: No action is required from the customer; AEMaaCS autoscaling is fully Adobe-managed and Adobe's orchestration engine will scale the Publish tier automatically in response to traffic increases.

questions.rationale: D is correct because AEMaaCS autoscaling is 100% Adobe-managed — customers cannot configure scaling rules, minimum pod counts, scale-lock windows, or thresholds for any tier in any environment [3]. The orchestration engine continuously monitors service load and scales horizontally (pod count) and vertically (resource allocation) automatically. A is incorrect because no such scaling settings UI exists in Cloud Manager for customers; scaling controls are internal to Adobe's orchestration infrastructure. B is incorrect because while Adobe Support can be engaged, pre-configuring autoscaling thresholds is not a supported request — the orchestration engine is not customer-configurable even via support. C is incorrect because Cloud Manager Config pipelines deploy YAML files for CDN rules, log forwarding, and similar configurations; they do not include any mechanism for customers to set pod count or scaling policies. For event preparedness, the correct approach is to trust Adobe's auto-scaling and optionally perform load testing to confirm behavior.

questions.topic: Audit Log and Log Forwarding Limitations
questions.scenario: A financial services company using AEM as a Cloud Service must satisfy a compliance audit requiring a 2-year retention of all repository change events (page edits, asset deletions, replication events) as well as user permission changes. The compliance team assumes all this data can be captured via the `logForwarding.yaml` mechanism and forwarded to their SIEM. A junior engineer has been asked to implement this forwarding.
questions.question: Which statement correctly describes the limitations the engineer will encounter, and what approach addresses the compliance requirement?

options.label: A
options.text: The `aemaccess` log captures all repository changes and user permission events, and can be forwarded to any supported SIEM destination via `logForwarding.yaml` to satisfy both requirements.

options.label: B
options.text: The AEM audit log at `/var/audit` is a JCR node store and cannot be forwarded via `logForwarding.yaml`; additionally, it does not capture ACL or user management changes. Satisfying both requirements needs the audit log forwarded via a separate mechanism (Adobe I/O Events) combined with an external Identity Provider audit trail for permission changes.

options.label: C
options.text: All audit data including user permission changes is automatically included in the `aemerror` log stream and will be forwarded once `logForwarding.yaml` is deployed with the correct destination.

options.label: D
options.text: The `logForwarding.yaml` mechanism supports forwarding the JCR audit log to Splunk by setting `audit: enabled: true` under the data section; user permission events must be separately enabled in OSGi configuration.

questions.rationale: B is correct because there are two distinct limitations at play. First, the AEM audit log in AEMaaCS is not a flat file — it is a JCR node store at `/var/audit` and therefore cannot be forwarded to Splunk or any other external system via the standard `logForwarding.yaml` mechanism [5]. Second, even if it were accessible, the audit log does not track ACL changes or user/group management events by default — those require an external Identity Provider's audit trail [5]. For the full compliance picture, repository change events can be streamed via Adobe I/O Events (a separate mechanism outside `logForwarding.yaml`), while permission change auditing must come from the federated IdP (e.g., Azure AD audit logs). A is incorrect because `aemaccess` is an HTTP access log recording request/response pairs — it has no knowledge of JCR node-level operations or user management events. C is incorrect because `aemerror` captures Java application exceptions and log statements; audit events written to JCR nodes are not output to `aemerror`. D is incorrect because no `audit: enabled: true` flag exists in `logForwarding.yaml`; the schema supports AEM, Dispatcher, and CDN logs but not JCR audit data.

questions.topic: CDN Cache Headers and New Relic Alerting
questions.scenario: An AEM as a Cloud Service team is investigating a cache efficiency problem. CDN log analysis shows the cache hit ratio for HTML pages is approximately 35%, far below Adobe's recommended 90%+ threshold. An APM trace in New Relic shows HTML page response times are averaging 1.2 seconds. The team sets up a New Relic alert policy to trigger when cache hit ratio drops below 80% so they can respond proactively next time. Additionally, a content editor notices that after publishing a page, the CDN continues to serve the stale version for up to an hour — they want to force immediate content refresh at the CDN edge after every publish event.
questions.question: Which combination of actions correctly addresses all three concerns — improving cache hit ratio, enabling proactive alerting, and forcing immediate CDN refresh on publish?

options.label: A
options.text: Add `Vary: User-Agent` response headers to maximize cache personalization, create a New Relic alert policy in the AEMaaCS sub-account, and configure a Dispatcher Flush agent to invalidate CDN entries on replication.

options.label: B
options.text: Strip marketing query parameters (`utm_*`) via CDN rules in `cdn.yaml` to normalize cache keys, create a New Relic alert policy in the AEMaaCS sub-account, and use a surrogate-key CDN PURGE call triggered by a replication post-processor to invalidate affected pages.

options.label: C
options.text: Strip marketing query parameters via CDN rules in `cdn.yaml`, set distinct CDN and browser TTLs using `Surrogate-Control` and `Cache-Control` headers on the Dispatcher vhost, accept that New Relic alerting is not available in the AEMaaCS-bundled tier and use an external alerting tool, and trigger a CDN PURGE API call with `X-AEM-Purge-Key` after publish to immediately invalidate stale content.

options.label: D
options.text: Configure autoscaling to add more Publish pods to reduce origin response time, accept that CDN TTLs cannot be changed without a full-stack deployment, and use the New Relic built-in dashboard to manually review cache metrics daily.

questions.rationale: C is correct because it accurately addresses all three concerns without contradiction. For cache hit ratio: marketing query parameters like `utm_*` and `gclid` create unique cache keys per URL variant, dramatically reducing hit ratio; stripping them in `cdn.yaml` normalizes the cache key [8]. Setting `Surrogate-Control: max-age=<longer>` on the Dispatcher vhost lets the CDN cache HTML longer than the browser does, improving edge hit ratio without stale browser-side content [6]. For alerting: the AEMaaCS-bundled New Relic sub-account explicitly does NOT support alert policies — alerting is excluded from the read-only tier [1][2]. The correct answer acknowledges this limitation and routes to an external tool. For CDN invalidation: the CDN PURGE API with `X-AEM-Purge-Key` is the mechanism for programmatically invalidating CDN-cached resources immediately after publish [7]. A is incorrect on two grounds: `Vary: User-Agent` dramatically worsens cache hit ratio (creates unique cache entries per browser user-agent string) and New Relic alerting is not available in the AEMaaCS sub-account. B is partially correct (query param stripping and PURGE API are valid) but is wrong about New Relic alert policies — they cannot be created in the AEMaaCS-bundled sub-account. D is incorrect because autoscaling is Adobe-managed and customer-unconfigurable, CDN TTLs can be changed via Config pipeline without a full-stack deployment, and manual daily review is not proactive alerting.

questions.scenario: A developer is reviewing the /filter section of a publish farm and finds the following two rules in sequence: rule /0050 { /type "deny" /url "/content/internal/*" } followed by rule /0060 { /type "allow" /url "/content/*" }. A request arrives for /content/internal/employees.html.
questions.question: What response will the Dispatcher return for this request?

options.label: A
options.text: 404 Not Found, because the deny rule at /0050 matches the request first.

options.label: B
options.text: The request is allowed and forwarded to the publish tier, because rule /0060 is the last matching rule.

options.label: C
options.text: 404 Not Found, because the /content/internal/* pattern is more specific than /content/* and always takes precedence.

options.label: D
options.text: The request is blocked and returns a 403 Forbidden response.

questions.rationale: B is correct because AEM Dispatcher uses last-match-wins semantics for filter evaluation [1] — all matching rules are evaluated in sequence and the last one that matches determines the outcome. Both rules match /content/internal/employees.html, but /0060 (allow) is evaluated after /0050 (deny), so the final decision is allow. A is incorrect because Dispatcher does NOT use first-match-wins logic like Apache mod_rewrite; it continues evaluating all rules after the first match. C is incorrect because specificity is not a factor — rule ordering and last-match-wins is the only evaluation mechanism. D is incorrect because Dispatcher returns 404 for denied requests, not 403; and in this case the request is actually allowed by the final matching rule.

questions.scenario: A content editor publishes a page at /content/mysite/en/about.html. The Dispatcher has statfileslevel set to 2 and the docroot is /var/www/html. The Dispatcher cache contains pages from several different language paths: /content/mysite/en/, /content/mysite/fr/, and /content/mysite/de/.
questions.question: Which cached files will be marked as stale following this activation?

options.label: A
options.text: Only /content/mysite/en/about.html is invalidated; other paths are unaffected.

options.label: B
options.text: All files in /content/mysite/ across all language paths are invalidated because the .stat file at /content/mysite/ is touched.

options.label: C
options.text: All files under /content/mysite/en/ are invalidated; /fr/ and /de/ caches remain valid.

options.label: D
options.text: The entire Dispatcher cache is invalidated because any activation triggers a full cache flush.

questions.rationale: C is correct because statfileslevel 2 creates .stat files at docroot (level 0), /content (level 1), and /content/mysite (level 2) — but the activation of a page under /content/mysite/en/ touches only the .stat file at /content/mysite/en/ and above [1][3]. Any cached file older than the touched .stat files in its directory path is considered stale. Since /fr/ and /de/ have their own .stat files at level 3 (below the touched level 2 .stat file), they are unaffected. A is incorrect because statfileslevel invalidates all files in the same directory subtree relative to the stat file, not just the single activated file. B is incorrect because statfileslevel 2 isolates invalidation at the /content/mysite/<lang>/ level — one language's update does not cascade to sibling language directories. D is incorrect because a statfileslevel 0 configuration would cause full cache invalidation; statfileslevel 2 is specifically designed to prevent this.

questions.scenario: During a security audit of an AEMaaCS publish Dispatcher configuration, a reviewer finds the following filter rule as the only allow rule for content paths: /0020 { /type "allow" /url "/content/app/*" }. The company's web application uses Sling selectors heavily (e.g., /content/app/page.model.json) and also uses URL suffixes for pagination. The security team reports that the current filter configuration is a potential DoS vector.
questions.question: What is the most secure way to address the security concern while maintaining application functionality?

options.label: A
options.text: Add a deny rule for known dangerous selectors like .infinity.json after the allow rule to block specific attack vectors.

options.label: B
options.text: Replace the broad allow rule with a precise rule that specifies allowed selectors and extensions explicitly, and add default-deny rules for all selectors and suffixes.

options.label: C
options.text: Switch from /url to /glob matching since /glob provides tighter control over the entire request line.

options.label: D
options.text: Enable the WAF rules in cdn.yaml to block malicious selector patterns at the CDN layer instead of at the Dispatcher.

questions.rationale: B is correct because the recommended approach per Adobe's Dispatcher Optimizer Tool (DOT) guidelines [3] is to add default-deny rules for all Sling selectors (/selectors "*" /type "deny") and all Sling suffixes (/suffix "*" /type "deny"), then explicitly allowlist only the selectors and suffixes the application needs (DOTRules:Disp-6 and Disp-7). This prevents attackers from crafting arbitrary selector/suffix combinations that bypass cache and overwhelm the publish tier. A is incorrect because a denylist of known dangerous patterns (blacklisting) will always be incomplete — new attack vectors are constantly discovered. The recommended approach is an allowlist. C is incorrect because /glob is deprecated since Dispatcher 4.2.0 [1] and provides coarser, less secure matching than the granular URL-component properties. Switching to /glob would make the configuration less secure, not more. D is incorrect because WAF rules at the CDN layer provide a complementary defense-in-depth layer, but the root cause — an overly permissive Dispatcher filter — must be fixed at the Dispatcher layer. Dispatcher is the first line of defense for the publish tier and should not rely solely on CDN-layer filtering.

questions.topic: Dispatcher Cache Invalidation — Flush Agent Placement
questions.scenario: A team is experiencing intermittent cache issues on their AEM 6.5 on-premise environment. After a content editor activates a page, users sometimes see the old cached version from the Dispatcher for several seconds and then occasionally see the old version get cached again permanently. The current setup has a Dispatcher Flush Agent configured on the Author instance. The Dispatcher cache is correctly invalidated, but re-fetches from the Publish tier occasionally return stale data.
questions.question: What is the root cause of this behavior, and what is the correct remediation?

options.label: A
options.text: The /gracePeriod is set too low. Increase it to at least 30 seconds to give Publish time to receive and index the content.

options.label: B
options.text: The Author-side Flush Agent creates a race condition where the Dispatcher cache is invalidated before replication to Publish completes. Move the Flush Agent to the Publish instance.

options.label: C
options.text: The /statfileslevel is too low. Increasing it will ensure all related pages are invalidated, preventing the stale version from being re-served.

options.label: D
options.text: Set /serveStaleOnError "1" in the farm configuration so the Dispatcher serves the cached copy when Publish returns an error during re-validation.

questions.rationale: B is correct because when a Flush Agent runs on Author, the invalidation request reaches the Dispatcher immediately after (or even before) replication to Publish has completed [2][3]. The Dispatcher then re-fetches the page from Publish, which may still be serving the old version, and permanently caches that stale version. Moving the Flush Agent to the Publish instance ensures the flush only fires after the new content is fully available on Publish — this is Adobe's recommended pattern [2]. A is incorrect because /gracePeriod controls how long an already-stale file can continue to be served during burst activations. It does not affect the replication-flush timing window and increasing it would make the problem worse, not better. C is incorrect because the issue is not about which pages are invalidated, but about a timing race where the Dispatcher fetches from a not-yet-updated Publish instance. statfileslevel tuning addresses invalidation scope, not sequencing. D is incorrect because /serveStaleOnError is designed for origin unavailability (5xx errors), not for the race condition where the origin returns a successful but stale response. It would not prevent the stale content from being permanently re-cached.

questions.scenario: A DevOps engineer on an AEMaaCS project needs to customize the default cache rules for a new microsite. After reviewing the directory structure, they notice `conf.dispatcher.d/cache/default_rules.any` and `conf.dispatcher.d/cache/rules.any` both exist. They modify `default_rules.any` to add the new caching rules and push to Cloud Manager. The pipeline fails at the Build Images step.
questions.question: Why did the pipeline fail, and what is the correct approach?

options.label: A
options.text: The farm file must be updated to reference `default_rules.any` explicitly before `rules.any` using a $include directive. The include order was missing.

options.label: B
options.text: `default_rules.any` is an immutable file. Phase 3 of Cloud Manager's validation detects the modification and fails the build. Customizations must be made in `rules.any`, the mutable counterpart.

options.label: C
options.text: AEMaaCS does not support custom cache rules. Cache behavior must be controlled through Cache-Control headers from AEM Publish instead of Dispatcher configuration.

options.label: D
options.text: The project is using legacy mode. Migrate to flexible mode by adding `opt-in/USE_SOURCES_DIRECTLY`, which will allow modifications to `default_rules.any`.

questions.rationale: B is correct because `default_rules.any` is one of the immutable (Adobe-managed) files in the Dispatcher configuration [1][3]. Cloud Manager's Build Images step runs a three-phase validation; Phase 3 is an immutability check that detects any modifications to these files and fails the pipeline. The engineer should place custom cache rules in `rules.any` (the mutable counterpart) and ensure the farm file includes both files in the correct order: defaults first, then overrides. A is incorrect because the question describes a build failure, which is a Phase 3 immutability check failure, not a missing include directive. The $include order is a valid concern but not the root cause here. C is incorrect because Dispatcher absolutely supports custom cache rules via the mutable `rules.any` file. AEMaaCS does not remove caching flexibility — it enforces it through the mutable/immutable file boundary. D is incorrect because the mutable/immutable distinction exists in both flexible mode and legacy mode. Migrating to flexible mode does not make immutable files editable — it only removes file count and size restrictions for mutable files. Modifying `default_rules.any` will still fail Phase 3 validation in flexible mode.

questions.scenario: An AEMaaCS team wants HTML pages to be cached at the Adobe-managed CDN for 12 hours but only cached in browsers for 5 minutes. They configure a vhost LocationMatch directive that sets `Cache-Control: max-age=43200` and also sets `Surrogate-Control: max-age=300`. After deploying, they observe browsers are caching pages for 12 hours and the CDN is only caching pages for 5 minutes — the opposite of their intention.
questions.question: What is the correct configuration to achieve a 12-hour CDN TTL and a 5-minute browser TTL?

options.label: A
options.text: Set `Cache-Control: max-age=300` and `Surrogate-Control: max-age=43200`. The CDN uses Surrogate-Control when present, leaving Cache-Control for browsers.

options.label: B
options.text: Set `Cache-Control: max-age=300, s-maxage=43200`. The s-maxage directive overrides max-age for shared caches (CDNs) while browsers use max-age.

options.label: C
options.text: Set `Cache-Control: max-age=43200` only. The CDN will honor the max-age and browsers will be told to revalidate every 5 minutes via the Age header.

options.label: D
options.text: Remove Surrogate-Control and set `Cache-Control: max-age=300` with `Vary: Surrogate-Capability` to differentiate CDN and browser caching.

questions.rationale: A is correct because when both `Cache-Control` and `Surrogate-Control` are present, the Adobe-managed CDN (Fastly) prioritizes `Surrogate-Control` for its own TTL and strips it before passing the response to the browser [1][2]. This means the browser only sees `Cache-Control: max-age=300` (5 minutes) while the CDN uses `Surrogate-Control: max-age=43200` (12 hours). The team had the values swapped — they set `Cache-Control: max-age=43200` (which browsers use) and `Surrogate-Control: max-age=300` (which the CDN uses), producing the opposite of the desired behavior. B is incorrect because while `s-maxage` is a valid standard mechanism to set CDN-only TTL, when `Surrogate-Control` is also present, the Adobe CDN prefers `Surrogate-Control` over `s-maxage` [1][3]. Since the scenario is AEMaaCS with Adobe's managed CDN, `Surrogate-Control` is the recommended approach. Using only `s-maxage` would work in this context if Surrogate-Control were absent, but the explanation of browser vs CDN header behavior is what makes A the canonical answer. C is incorrect because there is no mechanism by which the `Age` header instructs browsers to revalidate on a shorter cycle than `max-age`. `Age` indicates how long the cached response has already been held — it does not set a browser-side revalidation interval. D is incorrect because `Vary: Surrogate-Capability` is an ESI-related header used in Varnish/Fastly ESI setups, not a mechanism for separating browser and CDN TTLs.

questions.topic: Dispatcher Local Validation and Immutable Files — Cross-Domain
questions.scenario: An AEMaaCS DevOps engineer runs `./bin/validate.sh ./src` locally after making Dispatcher changes. All three phases pass. They commit and push to Cloud Manager. The Full-Stack Pipeline fails at the Build Images step with error: 'Immutability check failed: default_filters.any has been modified.' The engineer is confused because Phase 3 passed locally. Upon investigation, they find their local Dispatcher SDK version is 2021.1.3470, while Cloud Manager is using SDK version 2021.11.6126.
questions.question: What is the most likely explanation for the Phase 3 discrepancy, and what is the correct remediation?

options.label: A
options.text: The Cloud Manager build runs a stricter version of Phase 3 that cannot be replicated locally. Submit a support ticket to Adobe to relax the immutability check for this project.

options.label: B
options.text: The local SDK has an older baseline for `default_filters.any`. Phase 3 passed locally because the local checksum matched the old baseline. Cloud Manager uses the newer SDK baseline and detects the divergence. Run `bin/update_maven.sh` to sync local immutable files and checksums, review the changes, then recommit.

options.label: C
options.text: The engineer should add `opt-in/USE_SOURCES_DIRECTLY` to activate flexible mode, which removes the Phase 3 immutability check from both local and Cloud Manager validation.

options.label: D
options.text: The engineer should copy `default_filters.any` from the Cloud Manager-downloaded SDK version into the project to replace the locally modified copy, bypassing the checksum mismatch.

questions.rationale: B is correct. The root cause is a version mismatch between the local Dispatcher SDK and the one Cloud Manager uses [4][5]. The local `pom.xml` contains checksums corresponding to the older SDK baseline (2021.1.3470), so `validate.sh` compares the current `default_filters.any` against the old expected checksum — and it passes. Cloud Manager uses the newer SDK baseline (2021.11.6126) in which `default_filters.any` has been updated with new content; the engineer's copy no longer matches this newer expected checksum, so Phase 3 fails. The correct fix is `bin/update_maven.sh src/dispatcher` [4][5], which refreshes the local immutable files to the current SDK baseline and updates `pom.xml` checksums, then a careful review to ensure custom filter rules in `filters.any` still complement the updated defaults. A is incorrect because Phase 3 is deliberately designed to be identical between local and Cloud Manager validation — the SDK download replicates the exact Cloud Manager environment. A support ticket to relax immutability checks would be denied; the check is a security and correctness requirement. C is incorrect because flexible mode does not remove the Phase 3 immutability check. Flexible mode only removes file-count and file-size restrictions for mutable customizable files. Immutable files remain immutable in both modes [2][4]. D is incorrect because manually copying the file would create a new version mismatch — the copied file would match Cloud Manager's baseline content, but the `pom.xml` checksums in the project still expect the older version, so the build would still fail on checksum verification. The correct tool is `update_maven.sh`, which handles both the file sync and the `pom.xml` checksum update atomically.

questions.scenario: An AEMaaCS team runs a high-traffic news website. Their Dispatcher uses `enableTTL "1"` with Dispatcher version 4.2.9, and all HTML pages are served with `Cache-Control: max-age=3600`. The team also has a Dispatcher Flush Agent on the Publish instance. After deploying a critical breaking-news page update, editors observe that: (1) the newly activated page shows fresh content, (2) but 15 related sidebar pages that reference the updated page's title still show the old title. The editors triggered a manual flush of the updated page only, using `CQ-Action-Scope: ResourceOnly` in the flush request.
questions.question: What combination of factors explains why the sidebar pages show stale data, and what change should the team make to their invalidation architecture?

options.label: A
options.text: The ResourceOnly flush bypassed .stat file updates, leaving the sidebar pages' cached files unaffected. Remove the ResourceOnly header so the flush touches the .stat file hierarchy, invalidating related pages.

options.label: B
options.text: Dispatcher version 4.2.9 with enableTTL causes the Dispatcher to ignore .stat-based flush invalidation entirely for TTL-governed files. Since the TTL hasn't expired and .stat invalidation is bypassed, sibling pages remain stale until their 1-hour TTL expires. Upgrade Dispatcher to 4.3.5+ where both TTL and stat-file invalidation are respected.

options.label: C
options.text: The Publish-side Flush Agent fires before CDN purge completes. The CDN is still serving cached sidebar pages from its edge. Add a CDN hard purge via the PURGE API after each flush to clear the CDN cache.

options.label: D
options.text: The /statfileslevel is set too high, which prevents the .stat file from being touched at the correct directory depth for the sidebar pages. Lower statfileslevel to 0 to ensure all content is invalidated.

questions.rationale: B is correct and requires understanding the interaction between two concepts. First, Dispatcher versions before 4.3.5 with `enableTTL` enabled cause the Dispatcher to use ONLY TTL expiry for cache validation — it completely ignores `.stat`-file-based flush invalidation for TTL-governed files [4][5]. This means the Publish-side Flush Agent sends the invalidation signal, the .stat files are touched, but the Dispatcher ignores them because TTL governs the files and the TTL (1 hour) has not expired. Second, the `CQ-Action-Scope: ResourceOnly` flush correctly targeted only the activated page — it would not have invalidated the sidebar pages even if stat-file invalidation were working, since ResourceOnly explicitly bypasses .stat file updates [1][2]. Both factors compound: even if the team removes ResourceOnly (addressing the second factor), the 4.2.9 + enableTTL combination means stat-file invalidation is still ignored. The complete fix is upgrading to Dispatcher 4.3.5+, which respects both mechanisms. A is incorrect in its diagnosis of the root cause. While it is true that ResourceOnly flushes bypass .stat file updates and thus sidebar pages would not be invalidated by the flush, this is only half the story. The deeper issue is the Dispatcher 4.2.9 + enableTTL combination, which means even a full (non-ResourceOnly) flush would not invalidate the TTL-governed sidebar pages. Removing ResourceOnly alone would not solve the problem on Dispatcher 4.2.9. C is incorrect because the scenario describes a Dispatcher-layer caching problem specifically — the editors observe stale sidebar content even on subsequent requests, which indicates the Dispatcher is serving from its own cache, not the CDN. CDN layer issues would also manifest as stale content, but the root cause here is the Dispatcher 4.2.9 + enableTTL behavior. D is incorrect because lowering statfileslevel to 0 would cause the entire cache to be invalidated on every activation (a serious performance regression on a high-traffic news site) and would not address the fundamental issue that Dispatcher 4.2.9 + enableTTL ignores stat-file invalidation entirely for TTL-governed files.
